symbols: dead body of Christ stretched out — the completed sacrifice of the cross
three crosses in background — Golgotha, the place of crucifixion
weeping women — the grief of those who loved Christ most intimately
Virgin Mary in dark veil — the mother's sorrow at the sword that pierced her soul
crown of thorns — the mockery of kingship now transformed into true glory
wounds visible on the body — the price of redemption
description: A horizontal composition showing the dead body of Christ laid out in the foreground, surrounded by a large group of mourners. Christ's body is pale and limp, with visible wounds and a crown of thorns. The Virgin Mary in a white headdress and dark robes leans over him in anguish. A woman with flowing hair (Mary Magdalene) weeps at Christ's feet. Other figures — men and women — stand behind in various poses of grief. Three crosses are visible against a cloudy sky in the background. The emotional intensity of the scene is palpable in the gestures and expressions of the mourners.
theological_description: The Lamentation is the moment between the Deposition from the Cross and the Burial — a sacred pause in which the Church contemplates the reality of Christ's death. The horizontal arrangement of Christ's body, surrounded by mourners, creates a visual anticipation of the altar: the dead body is the sacrificial offering that will be commemorated in every Eucharistic celebration. The Virgin Mary's grief fulfills Simeon's prophecy that 'a sword will pierce through your own soul also' (Luke 2:35). Mary Magdalene at the feet recalls her earlier anointing of those same feet with her tears (Luke 7:38), now weeping not in penitence but in desolation. The three crosses visible in the background anchor the scene at Golgotha and remind the viewer that Christ did not die alone but between two thieves — even in death, surrounded by sinners. The visible wounds on Christ's body are not merely marks of suffering but the price of redemption: 'by his wounds we are healed' (Isaiah 53:5). The entire scene invites the viewer to enter into the grief before the joy of resurrection, understanding that the cross is not a detour but the very path of salvation.

scripture_refs.reason: Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus taking down the body and preparing it for burial
The women who had followed Jesus from Galilee watching where the body was laid
Simeon's prophecy that a sword would pierce Mary's soul — fulfilled in her grief at the cross
By his wounds we are healed — the visible wounds on Christ's body as the marks of redemption
Mary and the beloved disciple at the cross — the intimate witnesses to Christ's death
